summary: Nephi begins his record during the reign of Zedekiah. Lehi sees a vision of a pillar of fire and a book, learning of Jerusalem's impending destruction. He prophesies to the Jews, who mock him.
Warned by the Lord, Lehi takes his family into the wilderness, leaving his possessions. Laman and Lemuel murmur. Nephi prays and is visited by the Lord, who promises him a land of inheritance.
Lehi's sons return to Jerusalem to obtain the brass plates from Laban. After two failed attempts and physical abuse from Laman and Lemuel, an angel appears to rebuke the elder brothers.
Nephi, led by the Spirit, enters Jerusalem and finds Laban drunk. Constrained by the Spirit, he kills Laban, obtains the brass plates, and convinces Laban's servant Zoram to join them.
The sons return to Lehi and Sariah in the wilderness. Sariah is comforted and gains a testimony. Lehi searches the plates, discovering his genealogy as a descendant of Joseph.
Nephi explains that his record is intended to persuade men to come unto God and that he will not focus on genealogies but on the things of God.
Lehi's sons return to Jerusalem to bring Ishmael's family into the wilderness. Rebellion breaks out on the journey back, but Nephi is delivered by faith and the brothers repent.
Lehi recounts his vision of the Tree of Life, including the path, the rod of iron, and the great and spacious building. Laman and Lemuel refuse to partake of the fruit.
Nephi describes the two sets of plates he is making: the Small Plates for spiritual matters and the Large Plates for secular history, according to the Lord's command.
Lehi prophesies of the Messiah's coming in 600 years, the ministry of John the Baptist, the scattering and gathering of Israel, and the necessity of the Holy Ghost.
Nephi is caught away in vision and sees the meaning of his father's dream. He beholds the Virgin Mary, the birth and ministry of the Son of God, and the 12 Apostles.
Nephi sees the future of his seed in the promised land, including the visit of the resurrected Christ, centuries of peace, and the eventual destruction of his people due to pride.
The vision shows the formation of a great and abominable church, the colonization of America, and the restoration of plain and precious truths through the Book of Mormon.
Nephi learns there are only two churches: the Church of the Lamb and the Church of the Devil. He sees the final triumph of Zion and the work of John the Revelator.
Nephi returns to the tent and finds his brothers disputing. He explains the prophecies of the olive tree and the symbols in Lehi's vision, warning of God's justice.
Nephi's brothers are humbled by his words. The family finds the Liahona. Journeying south, Nephi breaks his bow, leading to a crisis of faith that is resolved by his obedience.
After eight years in the wilderness, the family reaches the land Bountiful. Nephi is commanded to build a ship. Despite his brothers' mockery, he demonstrates God's power over them.
The ship is finished and the family sets sail. Jacob and Joseph are born. Laman and Lemuel rebel on the ship, but a storm forces them to repent. They arrive in the promised land.
Nephi makes the Small Plates and records the prophecies of Zenock, Neum, and Zenos concerning the Messiah's suffering and the scattering of Israel.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 48 to his brothers, emphasizing Israel's stubbornness and the Lord's power to refine and deliver them from captivity.
Nephi continues quoting Isaiah (chapter 49), prophesying of the Lord's servant and the gathering of Israel from the ends of the earth.
Nephi explains the Isaiah passages, describing the restoration of the gospel in the last days, the binding of Satan, and the ultimate salvation of the righteous.
Lehi speaks his final words to his family in the promised land. He prophesies that the land will be one of liberty if they are righteous, but they will be scattered if they fall into unbelief.

Lehi teaches his son Jacob about the fundamental principles of the plan of salvation, including the necessity of opposition in all things, the Fall of Adam, and the redemption through the Holy Messiah.
Lehi blesses his son Joseph and recounts the prophecies of Joseph of Egypt. These prophecies foretell a choice seer (Joseph Smith) who will bring forth the Lord's word in the latter days.
After Lehi dies and is buried, Laman and Lemuel's anger toward Nephi rekindles. Nephi records a poetic prayer (the Psalm of Nephi), expressing deep sorrow for his sins and praising God's mercy.
Warned by the Lord, Nephi flees into the wilderness with those who will follow him, separating from Laman and Lemuel. They establish the land of Nephi, build a temple, and live in happiness.
Jacob begins a sermon by citing the prophecies of Isaiah. He explains the scattering of the Jews at Jerusalem and their eventual return and restoration when they believe in the Messiah.
Jacob quotes Isaiah 50. The Lord asks where the bill of divorcement is for Israel, explaining that they sold themselves through iniquity, yet His hand is not shortened that He cannot redeem.
Jacob quotes Isaiah 51 and 52. The Lord promises to comfort Zion and make her desert like Eden. He commands Zion to awake and put on her strength, promising that the redeemed shall return.
Jacob delivers a profound discourse on the Atonement. He explains how Christ's infinite sacrifice overcomes both temporal and spiritual death, and he warns against various sins and pride.

Jacob continues his sermon, prophesying that Christ's name was revealed to him by an angel. He promises that the Americas will be a land of liberty for the Gentiles and a place of inheritance for Israel.
Nephi introduces more of Isaiah's writings. He expresses his delight in proving the truth of Christ's coming and uses the testimonies of Isaiah and Jacob as two additional witnesses to his own.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 2. In the last days, the mountain of the Lord's house will be established. The pride of man will be humbled, and the Lord alone will be exalted when He comes to judge.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 3. Jerusalem and Judah are punished for their wickedness. The social order collapses, and the daughters of Zion are rebuked for their vanity and haughtiness.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 4. After the day of judgment, the branch of the Lord will be beautiful. Zion will be purged of filth, and the Lord will provide a cloud and fire as a defense and refuge.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 5. The Lord's vineyard (Israel) is laid waste because it brought forth wild grapes instead of good fruit. Woes are pronounced upon the greedy, the drunken, and the proud.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 6. Isaiah sees the Lord in the temple. A seraphim purges his sins with a live coal. Isaiah is called to preach to a hard-hearted people until the land is desolate.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 7. During a war against Jerusalem, Isaiah tells King Ahaz to ask for a sign. The sign of Immanuel (the virgin birth) is given as proof of God's presence and eventual deliverance.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 8. Isaiah's son is a sign of the Assyrian invasion. The people are warned to fear the Lord rather than human confederacies and to seek the law and the testimony.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 9. A great light will shine upon those in darkness. A child is born who is the Prince of Peace. However, because the people do not repent, the Lord's anger is not turned away.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 10. The king of Assyria is used as a rod of the Lord's anger but is then punished for his own pride. A remnant of Israel will return and stay upon the Holy One.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 11. The Rod out of the stem of Jesse (Christ) will judge in righteousness. The Millennium will bring peace between animals, and the Lord will gather Israel a second time.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 12. A short hymn of praise and gratitude to the Lord Jehovah. The people are exhorted to draw water from the wells of salvation and declare God's doings among the people.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 13. The destruction of Babylon is foretold, serving as a type for the destruction of the world at the Second Coming. The day of the Lord will be cruel with wrath and anger.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 14. Israel will have rest and take captive their oppressors. A taunt is taken up against the king of Babylon, and the fall of Lucifer from heaven is recounted.

Nephi provides a commentary on Isaiah, stating his delight in plainness. He prophesies that the Jews will return to Jerusalem, crucify the Messiah, and then be scattered until they believe in Jesus Christ.

Nephi foresees the destruction of his people after Christ's visit. He warns against priestcrafts and secret combinations but emphasizes that the Lord's grace is free to all, regardless of race or status.

Nephi prophesies of the last days and the coming forth of a sealed book. He describes the encounter between the learned man and the unlearned man (Joseph Smith) regarding the translation of the record.

Nephi exposes the false doctrines and pride of the last days. He warns of the devil's tactics: stirring people to anger, lulling them into carnal security, and flattering them into believing there is no hell.
The Lord addresses the Gentiles who reject the Book of Mormon. He asserts His right to speak to all nations and that the testimony of two nations proves He is God.
Nephi explains that all who repent are the covenant people. He prophesies the restoration of the Lamanites and Jews through the Book of Mormon and describes the peace of the Millennial era.

Nephi explains the doctrine of Christ. He recounts the baptism of Jesus as an example of fulfilling all righteousness and outlines the path: faith, repentance, baptism, and the gift of the Holy Ghost.

Nephi teaches that after receiving the Holy Ghost, one can speak with the tongue of angels. He exhorts his people to feast upon the words of Christ and pray always so God will consecrate their efforts.
Nephi writes his final testimony. He acknowledges his weakness in writing but knows his words are true and will stand as a witness at the last day. He bids a final farewell to all mankind.
The signs of Christ's birth are given, including a day and a night and a day without darkness. Believers are saved from a plot by unbelievers to execute them, and a new star appears.
Wickedness increases as people begin to forget the signs. The Nephites and Lamanites unite against the Gadianton robbers, and the curse of dark skin is removed from converted Lamanites.
The Nephite governor Lachoneus receives a threatening letter from Giddianhi. The Nephites gather all their resources and people into one body for defense, led by the prophet-commander Gidgiddoni.
The Gadianton robbers fail in their siege and are defeated in battle. Giddianhi and his successor Zemnarihah are killed. The Nephites rejoice in God's deliverance and praise His name.
The people repent and establish peace. Mormon explains his lineage and the purpose of his abridgment, testifying of the Savior and the gathering of the House of Israel.
The Nephites prosper but fall into pride and social stratification. The church is broken up, and corrupt judges secretly murder prophets, leading to a conspiracy to establish a king.
The government is destroyed, and the people divide into tribes. A robber leader named Jacob becomes king of a wicked band. Nephi performs mighty miracles, even raising his brother from the dead.
As Christ is crucified in Jerusalem, catastrophic storms, earthquakes, and destructions ravage the Americas. Thick darkness covers the land for three days while the people mourn.
In the darkness, the voice of Jesus Christ is heard announcing the destruction of many cities. He explains that the law of Moses is fulfilled and invites all to come unto Him through repentance.
Silence prevails for many hours before Christ speaks again. The darkness and earthquakes cease. The more righteous survivors find that their lives were spared according to the words of the prophets.
The resurrected Jesus Christ appears to a multitude gathered at the temple in Bountiful. The people feel the wounds in His hands and feet. He gives authority to baptize and teaches its proper mode.
Christ calls twelve disciples and delivers a sermon similar to the Sermon on the Mount. He teaches the Beatitudes and explains how His laws fulfill and transcend the old law.
Christ continues His sermon, teaching about the inward nature of righteousness. He instructs the people on how to give alms, pray, and fast, and commands them to seek first the kingdom of God.
Christ warns against unrighteous judgment and hypocritical behavior. He teaches the importance of asking, seeking, and knocking, and characterizes the path to life as narrow.
Jesus declares that the law of Moses is fulfilled in Him. He explains that the Nephites are the 'other sheep' of whom He spoke to His disciples in Jerusalem, who would also hear His voice.
Christ speaks of other remnants of Israel who will yet hear His voice. He prophesies of the restoration of the gospel among the Gentiles and the eventual gathering of Israel in the last days.
Christ heals the sick and infirm among the multitude. He blesses the little children one by one and is surrounded by fire and angels in a scene of overwhelming love and holiness.
Jesus institutes the sacrament among the Nephites and gives instructions on prayer. He warns against partaking of the sacrament unworthily and gives authority to His disciples before ascending.
The twelve disciples teach and baptize the people. The Holy Ghost falls upon them. Jesus returns and offers three powerful prayers of intercession, demonstrating a supreme unity with the Father.

Jesus provides bread and wine miraculously and resumes His teachings. He quotes Isaiah and Micah, prophesying that the House of Israel will be gathered and established in the land of their inheritance.

The Savior identifies the coming forth of the Book of Mormon as the sign that the gathering has begun. He warns the Gentiles to repent or face the destruction that comes to those who reject the covenant.

Christ quotes Isaiah 54, comparing Israel to a woman who was forsaken but is now gathered with great mercies. He promises that Zion will be established in peace and no weapon against her shall prosper.
Christ commands the people to search the words of Isaiah diligently. He directs that the prophecy of Samuel the Lamanite concerning the resurrection of the saints be added to their records.
Jesus commands the people to record the words of Malachi 3. The prophecy concerns the messenger preparing the way and the Lord's sudden appearance at His temple to judge and refine His people.
Christ quotes Malachi 4, describing the day of burning that will leave the wicked without root or branch, while the Sun of Righteousness arises for the righteous. He promises to send Elijah.
Jesus expounds all things from the beginning. Children speak marvelous things that cannot be written. Mormon explains that he has only recorded a small portion of the Savior's teachings.

The disciples ask what to name the Church. Christ commands it be called after His name. He defines His Gospel as the mission of doing the Father's will and providing the way for repentance and salvation.
Nine of the disciples desire to return to Christ's kingdom, while three desire to tarry on earth to bring souls unto Him. Christ translates the three, granting them power to never taste of death.
Mormon provides a closing commentary, warning that those who spurn the Lord's work or deny His miracles in the last days will find themselves under the same condemnation as the wicked of old.
The Lord commands Mormon to write a final call to the Gentiles. He invites them to repent of their abominations, be baptized, and receive the Holy Ghost to be numbered with the House of Israel.

This book covers nearly 300 years of history following Christ's visitation. For nearly two centuries, the people live in perfect unity with all things common, and no divisions exist. Eventually, pride leads to social classes, the return of secret combinations, and a total rejection of the gospel.

Five different record keepers pass the small plates from one to another. The narrative covers several centuries, concluding with Amaleki's account of King Mosiah I leading the righteous Nephites to the land of Zarahemla, where they discover the descendants of Mulek and learn of the Jaredite survivor Coriantumr.

Enos recounts how his father's teachings led him to a day-long wrestle in prayer while hunting. He receives a remission of his sins through faith in Christ and then pours out his soul for the welfare of the Nephites and the Lamanites. The Lord promises the records will be preserved.

Mormon describes his discovery of the small plates of Nephi while abridging the large plates. He includes them for a wise purpose known to the Lord. He then bridges to King Benjamin's reign, his defense against the Lamanites, and the establishment of peace through holy prophets.

Nephi gives Jacob charge over the small plates and dies. A second king is appointed. Jacob and Joseph are consecrated priests. Jacob notes the onset of pride, materialism, and unchastity among the people.
Jacob delivers a powerful sermon at the temple condemning pride and greed, urging the people to share their substance. He delivers a strict warning against plural marriage and sexual immorality.
Jacob promises that the pure in heart will receive the word of God with gladness. He contrasts the Lamanites' fidelity in family with the Nephites' wickedness and warns the people to repent.
Jacob explains that he writes to lead his people to Christ. He notes that the Jews rejected the plainness of Christ and looked beyond the mark, causing them to stumble.
Jacob quotes Zenos's allegory of the tame and wild olive trees. The Master of the Vineyard and his servant work through stages of grafting and pruning to preserve the house of Israel before the end.
Jacob interprets Zenos's allegory, declaring that the last time has come for the Lord to labor in His vineyard. He pleads with Israel to repent and cleave unto God.

An anti-Christ named Sherem confronts Jacob, demanding a sign. The Lord strikes him, and after several days he confesses his deception and dies. The people are reclaimed, and Jacob passes the plates to Enos.
Moroni introduces the Jaredite record. At the Tower of Babel, Jared and his brother pray that their language not be confounded. The Lord promises to lead them to a choice land.
The group travels carrying seeds, flocks, and honey bees. After four years at the seashore, the Lord chastens the brother of Jared for neglecting prayer. He is instructed to build eight barges.
The brother of Jared moltens sixteen stones and asks the Lord to touch them for light. Because of his great faith, he sees the finger of the Lord and then the pre-mortal body of Jesus Christ.
Moroni records instructions to seal up the vision until the Restoration. Christ calls upon the Gentiles to repent and believe, promising manifestations of the Spirit to the faithful.
Moroni addresses the future translator, explaining that three witnesses will be shown the plates by the power of God to testify of their truthfulness.
The Jaredites put glowing stones in their barges and are driven by fierce wind for 344 days, singing praises. Upon landing, they establish a nation and choose Orihah as king.
Orihah reigns in righteousness, but later generations face civil war. Corihor rebels against his father Kib. Shule eventually restores the kingdom. Prophets warn of destruction.
Jared rebels against his father Omer. His daughter suggests using secret plans from Cain. Akish forms a secret combination to murder the king. Moroni warns that such combinations destroy nations.
Akish's secret combinations lead to widespread war. Omer flees for safety. Emer reigns in righteousness. Later, Heth brings back secret plans, leading to famine and poisonous serpents.
Contrasts tyranny under Riplakish with a golden age under King Lib where the people become exceedingly industrious and wealthy.
Many prophets warn of utter destruction, but they are rejected or killed. Successive kings reign in wickedness, and the nation is plagued by wars, famines, and pestilences.
Ether teaches that faith is things hoped for and not seen. Moroni fears his writing weakness. The Lord comforts him, stating that His grace is sufficient for the meek.
Ether prophesies of the New Jerusalem. Coriantumr is warned to repent or see his people destroyed. He rejects the warning, and a final bloody war begins.
Shiz wages a war of vengeance against Coriantumr. The conflict becomes so devastating that no one is left to bury the dead, and the scent of death covers the land.
All remaining people gather at the hill Ramah for a final battle. After days of slaughter, only Coriantumr and Shiz remain. Coriantumr decapitates Shiz, fulfilling Ether's prophecy.

Jarom describes a period of 238 years since leaving Jerusalem. While many Nephites are hard-hearted, the righteous prosper. Prophets and priests labor to teach the Law of Moses and persuade the people to look forward to the Messiah.
Following the death of Pahoran, his sons contend for the judgment seat. Pahoran II is murdered by Kishkumen. Coriantumr leads a Lamanite army to capture Zarahemla but is defeated by Moronihah.
Helaman is appointed chief judge. Kishkumen attempts to murder him but is killed. Gadianton organizes a secret band of robbers and flees into the wilderness.
Large numbers of Nephites migrate northward and build cities of cement. Helaman dies, and his son Nephi becomes chief judge. The Gadianton robbers remain a hidden threat.
Nephite dissenters and Lamanites conquer half of the Nephite lands. Moronihah acknowledges their losses are due to wickedness and pride. Nephi and Lehi begin to preach with power.
Nephi and Lehi are imprisoned but surrounded by fire and protected by God. A voice from heaven calls the people to repent, leading to the conversion of 8,000 Lamanites.
A period of great peace and prosperity ensues as righteous Lamanites preach to Nephites. However, the Gadianton robbers take over the Nephite government through murder and secret combinations.
Nephi returns to Zarahemla and finds the people in gross wickedness. He mourns upon a tower in his garden, attracting a crowd, and rebukes them for setting their hearts on riches and murder.
Corrupt judges attempt to incite the people against Nephi. He defends his words by citing testimonies of ancient prophets concerning Christ. He reveals the murder of the chief judge.
Five men find the chief judge Seezoram dead and are imprisoned. Nephi's prophecy is verified when he reveals that Seantum, the judge's brother, is the murderer.
The Lord commends Nephi's unwearied diligence and grants him the sealing power to bind and loose on earth and in heaven. He continues to preach but is rejected.
Nephi prays for a famine instead of war to humble the people. After a severe famine, the people repent and Nephi prays for rain. Prosperity returns but the people soon forget God.
Mormon provides a discourse on the unsteadiness and pride of man, explaining that unless the Lord chastens His people, they forget Him. The dust of the earth is more obedient than man.
Samuel the Lamanite stands on the walls of Zarahemla to preach. He prophesies of the sword of justice and warns that their riches are cursed because they have set their hearts on them.
Samuel prophesies of the signs of the Savior's birth (a night without darkness, a new star) and His death (three days of darkness, natural upheavals). He explains the purpose of the Atonement.
Samuel explains that the Lamanites are more blessed because of their firm faith. He warns the Nephites that they will be destroyed if they do not repent, having sinned against greater light.
Many believe Samuel and seek Nephi for baptism. Others try to kill Samuel with stones and arrows, but he is protected. Samuel flees back to his own land.

Ammaron charges ten-year-old Mormon to take the records. At eleven, Mormon moves to Zarahemla as war breaks out. Wickedness prevails and Mormon is visited by the Lord at fifteen but forbidden to preach.
Mormon leads the Nephite armies at sixteen. The people's mourning is not repentance but the sorrowing of the damned. Mormon retrieves the plates as commanded by Ammaron.
After a ten-year treaty, the Nephites swear vengeance. Mormon refuses to lead them any longer and stands as an idle witness of their destruction.
The Lamanites capture the city of Desolation and sacrifice Nephite women and children. The war becomes a scene of unprecedented carnage as both sides delight in bloodshed.
Mormon returns to lead without hope, describing the Nephites as chaff before the wind. He hides the records and prophesies of the Lamanites' and Gentiles' future roles.
The Nephites gather at Cumorah for a final battle. Hundreds of thousands are slain. Mormon hides the records and laments the fall of his fair people.
Mormon addresses the descendants of the Lamanites, urging them to believe in Jesus Christ, lay down their weapons, and follow the gospel to find rest in God's kingdom.
Moroni records his father's death and the final Nephite destruction. He describes the pride and secret combinations of the latter days, warning Gentiles against ignoring the poor.
Moroni speaks to those who deny Christ or miracles. He defines God as unchangeable and explains that miracles cease only through unbelief. He exhorts readers to come unto Christ.
Moroni, surviving alone, wanders for safety. He refuses to deny Christ despite Lamanites killing any Nephite who won't. He decides to write a few more things for future generations.
Moroni records Jesus Christ's specific words to His twelve disciples regarding conferring the gift of the Holy Ghost through the laying on of hands.
The procedure for ordaining priests and teachers is outlined — by the laying on of hands by those holding authority, in the name of Christ.
The exact sacramental prayer over the bread is recorded, emphasizing the covenant to take upon them the name of Christ and always remember Him.
The sacramental prayer over the wine is recorded, witnessing to God the Father that participants always remember His Son and may have His Spirit.
Guidelines for baptism and church membership: members nourished by the word of God, meeting together for fasting and prayer, led by the Spirit in worship.
Mormon's sermon on discerning good from evil, the necessity of faith for miracles, and the vital roles of hope and charity — the pure love of Christ.
Mormon's epistle declares infant baptism an abomination because little children are alive in Christ. Baptism is for those capable of committing sin.
Mormon's final epistle describes horrific brutality among Nephites and Lamanites. Despite the darkness, he exhorts Moroni to remain faithful and let Christ's grace rest upon him.
Moroni's famous promise: ask God with sincere heart and real faith to know the truth by the Holy Ghost. He discourses on spiritual gifts and bids a final farewell.
King Benjamin establishes peace and prepares his sons through the study of records. He confers the kingdom upon his son Mosiah and prepares the people for his final address.
Benjamin addresses his people from a tower, emphasizing his service as a king and that when we serve our fellow beings, we are serving God.
Benjamin recounts an angel's words concerning Christ's birth, ministry, and sacrifice. He teaches that the natural man is an enemy to God.
The people fall to the earth in humility and receive a remission of their sins. Benjamin exhorts them to impart of their substance to the poor.
The people declare they have no more disposition to do evil and take upon themselves the name of Christ through a covenant.
King Benjamin dies. Mosiah II reigns in righteousness. The names of those who entered the covenant are recorded.
Ammon leads an expedition to the land of Nephi and finds Limhi's people in bondage to the Lamanites. Limhi recounts the history of Zeniff and Abinadi.
Limhi asks Ammon to translate twenty-four gold plates. Ammon explains that King Mosiah is a seer with power to translate through divine instruments.
Zeniff's personal record begins. He leads a group from Zarahemla to settle in the land of Nephi, negotiating with King Laman but being deceived.
The Lamanites attack Zeniff's people. Zeniff leads his armies to victory, noting the Lamanites were stirred up by false traditions of their fathers.
King Noah succeeds Zeniff and reigns in wickedness, building spacious palaces and taxing the people. The prophet Abinadi is sent to warn them.
Abinadi returns to deliver a severe warning. He is captured and brought before Noah and his priests, who attempt to cross-examine him on scripture.
Abinadi is protected by divine power. He recites the Ten Commandments and teaches that the Law of Moses points to the Atonement.
Abinadi quotes Isaiah 53 regarding the Messiah's suffering, rejection, and final sacrifice for the sins of the world.
Abinadi explains the relationship between the Father and the Son. He prophesies of the resurrection and salvation of those who believe.
Abinadi concludes by declaring that Christ's redemption breaks the bands of death. He warns of the final judgment based on works.
Noah sentences Abinadi to death by fire. Alma, one of the priests, pleads for his life and is forced to flee. Abinadi is martyred.
Alma teaches Abinadi's words in secret and baptizes about two hundred souls at the Waters of Mormon, establishing the church.
King Noah's people are attacked by Lamanites. Noah is burned to death by his own people. His son Limhi becomes king under Lamanite subjugation.
Noah's priests kidnap Lamanite daughters, leading to war. Gideon helps resolve the misunderstanding, and the people return to bondage.
Limhi's people suffer and are defeated in three attempts to free themselves. They humble themselves and cry to the Lord. Ammon's expedition arrives.
Gideon devises a plan to escape. The Nephites get the Lamanite guards drunk and flee, eventually arriving safely in Zarahemla.
Alma and his followers settle in the land of Helam. They are discovered by Lamanites led by Amulon. The Lord warns Alma to prepare.
Amulon oppresses Alma's people and forbids prayer. The Lord eases their burdens and miraculously delivers them into the wilderness.
The groups of Limhi and Alma are united in Zarahemla. Mosiah II allows Alma to organize the church throughout the land.
The rising generation falls into unbelief. Alma seeks guidance from the Lord, who gives instructions on confession, forgiveness, and excommunication.
Alma the Younger and the sons of Mosiah persecute the church. An angel appears and rebukes them. After two days, Alma awakens declaring he is born again.
The sons of Mosiah decline the kingdom to serve a mission to the Lamanites. Mosiah translates the Jaredite plates using the Urim and Thummim.
Mosiah proposes government by judges instead of kings. The people accept, and Alma the Younger is appointed the first chief judge.
Nehor introduces priestcraft and kills Gideon; he is executed, but false doctrines and pride begin to infect the people.
Amlici seeks to be king and rebels; he joins with the Lamanites but is defeated and slain by Alma in personal combat.
The Amlicites mark themselves, fulfilling prophecy; Mormon discusses the curse and mark of the Lamanites.
Alma is grieved by pride and wickedness of the church; he resigns as chief judge to devote himself to preaching.
Alma's discourse to Zarahemla on the necessity of spiritual rebirth and following the Good Shepherd.
Alma sets the church in order in Zarahemla and proceeds to Gideon to continue his preaching mission.
Alma prophesies that Christ will be born of Mary and will take upon Himself the sins and infirmities of His people.
Alma is rejected in Ammonihah but returns at an angel's command; he is received by Amulek.
Alma warns the people of Ammonihah of their impending destruction if they do not repent.
Amulek provides his witness and rebukes the corrupt lawyers who seek to entrap them.
Amulek debates Zeezrom, testifying of Christ's coming and the universal resurrection of the dead.
Alma explains the plan of redemption, the state of probation, and conditions for entering the rest of the Lord.
Alma discourses on the high priesthood, the example of Melchizedek, and pre-mortal preparation of those called.
Believers in Ammonihah are burned; Alma and Amulek are imprisoned but delivered as the prison walls collapse.
Alma heals Zeezrom, who converts; the church is established in Sidom while the wicked remain unrepentant.
Lamanites destroy Ammonihah in a single day; Zoram leads Nephite armies to recover captives.
The sons of Mosiah journey to the Lamanites; Ammon becomes a servant to King Lamoni and defends his flocks.
Ammon's power leads Lamoni to inquire of the Great Spirit; Ammon teaches the gospel and the creation.
King Lamoni and his household are overwhelmed by the Spirit; the queen is converted and many Lamanites baptized.
Ammon and Lamoni encounter Lamoni's father; Ammon's courage causes the old king to soften and grant liberty.
Aaron preaches to the Amalekites but is imprisoned in Middoni; Ammon and Lamoni deliver them.
Aaron teaches Lamoni's father the plan of redemption; the king and household are converted; religious freedom decreed.
Thousands of Lamanites are converted and take the name Anti-Nephi-Lehi; they completely abandon their weapons.
The Anti-Nephi-Lehies refuse to fight invaders and are martyred; their examples lead to many more conversions.
Attacking Lamanites destroy the seed of Noah's priests as prophesied; many more Lamanites convert.
Ammon rejoices in the miraculous success of their mission and the great change wrought in the Lamanites.
The Lord commands the Anti-Nephi-Lehies to flee to the Nephites; they are given the land of Jershon.
A major war erupts. The Nephites suffer great loss but prevail. The chapter reflects on the unequal state of man.
Alma expresses his desire to cry repentance like an angel, yet acknowledges he should be content with his calling.
Korihor the anti-Christ preaches against God and Christ; he is struck dumb by Alma and eventually dies.
Alma leads a mission to reclaim the apostate Zoramites; he observes their prideful worship at the Rameumptom.
Alma teaches the humble Zoramites that faith begins as a seed which must be nourished to grow.
Alma quotes Zenos, Zenock, and Moses to show that the scriptures testify of Christ and the power of prayer.
Amulek testifies of the infinite atonement of Christ, which fulfills the law of Moses and brings salvation.
The Zoramite converts are cast out and received in Jershon; Zoramite leaders prepare for war.
Alma gives his personal conversion testimony to Helaman, describing his transition from agonizing guilt to exquisite joy.
Alma entrusts Helaman with the plates and the Liahona, emphasizing that by small things great things are brought to pass.
Alma counsels Shiblon to continue in steady faithfulness and to bridle his passions to be filled with love.
Alma rebukes Corianton for his sexual sin and abandonment of his mission, explaining the gravity of his actions.
Alma explains the spirit world and the resurrection, clarifying the state of the soul between death and rising.
Alma teaches the law of restoration — we are restored to the state of our desires, whether good or evil.
Alma explains that the atonement satisfies the demands of justice, allowing mercy to be extended to the repentant.
Moroni prepares the Nephites with armor and strategy to defend their liberty against Zerahemnah's Lamanites.
Moroni defeats Zerahemnah, requiring a covenant of peace; those who refuse are slain until they submit.
Alma prophesies the eventual destruction of the Nephite nation; he blesses his sons and departs.
Amalickiah conspires to be king; Moroni raises the Title of Liberty to rally the Nephites.
Amalickiah uses treachery and murder to become king of the Lamanites and launches war.
Moroni fortifies Nephite cities; Mormon compares Moroni's righteousness with Amalickiah's villainy.
Lamanites attack Ammonihah and Noah but are repelled by Moroni's fortifications; Amalickiah is wroth.
Moroni continues fortifications; Nephites experience unparalleled happiness and peace.
The king-men seek to overthrow the government; Moroni suppresses the revolt while Amalickiah captures cities.
Ammoron succeeds Amalickiah; Moroni and Teancum use a decoy strategy to retake the city of Mulek.
Nephite dissenters cause setbacks; the young sons of the Anti-Nephi-Lehies volunteer to fight under Helaman.
Moroni and Ammoron exchange fierce letters regarding prisoners and the cause of the war.
Moroni uses a stratagem involving wine to intoxicate Lamanite guards and free Nephite prisoners from Gid.
Helaman writes to Moroni, describing the miraculous protection of his two thousand stripling warriors.
The stripling warriors fight valiantly and are all preserved by God; Cumeni is retaken.
Helaman's army takes Manti by strategy despite being outnumbered; they give thanks for divine deliverance.
Moroni is grieved by the fall of Nephihah and blames the government for failure to support the armies.
Moroni writes a stinging epistle to Pahoran, rebuking him for supposed negligence and threatening to march against him.
Pahoran responds with humility, explaining he has been driven out by king-men rebels; he asks Moroni for aid.
Moroni joins Pahoran to suppress the rebellion and drive Lamanites from the land; Teancum kills Ammoron but is slain.
Hagoth builds ships for northward migration; Helaman and Shiblon die, and the records are passed to younger Helaman.
Abraham seeks the priesthood blessings despite his fathers' idolatry. He is nearly sacrificed on an altar but is delivered by Jehovah, who destroys the priest.
The Lord establishes the Abrahamic covenant, promising posterity, priesthood, and a gospel to bless all nations. Abraham travels to Canaan and Egypt.
Abraham receives a vision of the stars, Kolob, and the pre-mortal existence of spirits. The noble and great ones are shown as foreordained leaders.
The Gods plan and execute the creation of the heavens and the earth, organizing matter and establishing the conditions for mortal life.
The Gods complete the creation, form man from the dust, place him in the Garden of Eden, and create woman as his companion.
Joseph Smith recounts the religious confusion of his youth, the First Vision, Moroni's visits, obtaining the gold plates, and the restoration of the Aaronic Priesthood by John the Baptist.
Jesus foretells the destruction of Jerusalem and the signs of His Second Coming, warning His disciples to watch and be prepared for the great tribulations of the last days.
Moses is caught up into an exceedingly high mountain, where he sees God face to face and learns of his own divine potential as a son of God.
God reveals to Moses the account of the creation of the earth, emphasizing that all things were created spiritually before they were created physically.
The creation continues, detailing the physical formation of Adam and Eve, the placement of man in the Garden of Eden, and the first marriage.
The rebellion of Lucifer is described, followed by the temptation and fall of Adam and Eve and their expulsion from the Garden.
Adam and Eve offer sacrifices and receive the gospel; Cain rebels, makes a covenant with Satan, and murders his brother Abel.
The righteous lineage of Adam is preserved through Seth, and Enoch is called as a prophet to cry repentance to a wicked generation.
Enoch establishes the city of Zion, sees a vision of world history, and witnesses God and the heavens weeping over the wickedness of mankind.
As wickedness spreads, Noah is called to preach the gospel and warn the people of a coming flood if they do not repent.
Joseph Smith outlines thirteen fundamental points of doctrine and belief for the Church, covering the Godhead, the Fall, the Atonement, and core principles of the gospel.
King Josiah celebrates a great Passover in Jerusalem, but after his death, the kingdom falls into wickedness and is destroyed by the Babylonians.
Cyrus of Persia decrees that the Jews may return to rebuild the temple, but the work is halted by local officials.
Three young guardsmen in King Darius's court hold a contest to determine what is the strongest: wine, kings, or women.
Zerubbabel argues that Truth is the strongest of all, winning Darius's favor and securing the decree to rebuild Jerusalem.
A large group of exiles returns under Zerubbabel and begins to offer sacrifices and lay the foundation of the second temple.
Local governors challenge the building, prompting a search of Persian archives that reveals Cyrus's original decree.
The temple is completed and dedicated with great joy, followed by a Passover celebration.
Ezra the scribe receives authority from King Artaxerxes to lead another group of Jews back to Jerusalem and restore the law.
Ezra addresses intermarriage with foreign nations, leading the people to repent, separate, and listen to the law.
Antiochus Epiphanes desecrates the temple and brutally persecutes Jews who remain faithful to the law.
Mattathias refuses pagan sacrifices and starts a guerrilla war, ending with his death.
Judas Maccabeus wins dramatic victories over Syrian generals, giving hope to faithful Jews.
Judas cleanses the desecrated temple and institutes the Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah).
Maccabean forces rescue Jewish communities in Galilee and Gilead from hostile nations.
Antiochus dies in Persia; his successor besieges Jerusalem with a massive army including war elephants.
Demetrius sends Nicanor to destroy the Jews, but Judas defeats and kills him.
Judas learns of Rome's power and negotiates a treaty of mutual defense and friendship.
Judas Maccabeus is killed in battle; Jonathan takes command from the wilderness.
Rival kings compete for Jonathan's support, leading to his appointment as high priest.
Shifting alliances among kings lead to the rise of Demetrius II and the treacherous Trypho.
Jonathan strengthens ties with Rome and Sparta but is captured by Trypho.
Simon takes command, oversees Jonathan's burial, and negotiates full independence for Judea.
Judea enjoys a golden age of peace under Simon's leadership.
Antiochus VII grants Simon rights but later demands the return of captured cities.
Simon and two sons are murdered by his son-in-law Ptolemy; John Hyrcanus rises.
Ezra is commanded to deliver a message of judgment to Israel, declaring that God will forsake them and choose a new people because of their rebellion.
Ezra describes the gathering of the elect and the reward of the righteous, encouraging the people to wait for their Shepherd and receive their crowns.
In Babylon, Ezra offers a deeply personal prayer to God, lamenting the desolation of Zion and questioning why the wicked Babylonians are allowed to rule.
The angel Uriel answers Ezra's prayer by demonstrating that human understanding is insufficient to comprehend God's timing and the complexities of the world.
Uriel reveals the signs that will precede the end of the world, including social chaos, unnatural events, and the withdrawal of wisdom from the earth.
Ezra receives further revelation about the beginning and end of time, learning that the destruction of this age is necessary for the next age to begin.
Uriel explains the narrow way to salvation and describes the state of souls after death, including the finality of judgment for the wicked.
Ezra prays fervently for God's mercy on the weak, but Uriel explains that while God loves His creations, judgment must follow individual choice.
Uriel warns of coming tribulations for the world and Ezra encounters a mourning woman in a field who is grieving for her lost son.
The mourning woman is suddenly transformed into a glorious city, and Uriel explains that she represents the historical and future glory of Zion.
Ezra sees an apocalyptic vision of a massive eagle with three heads and many wings, representing successive world empires that will be destroyed.
A Lion appears and rebukes the Eagle; Uriel then explains that the Lion represents the Messiah who will deliver the remnant of God's people.
Ezra sees a Man rising from the sea who destroys a hostile multitude and gathers a peaceful group, representing the return of the ten tribes.
God calls to Ezra from a bush and instructs him to rewrite the lost scriptures, which he does by dictating ninety-four books to five scribes.
The Lord commands Ezra to write down prophecies of destruction for nations that have persecuted His people, describing coming plagues, wars, and famines.
The book concludes with a powerful call to repentance and a description of the great trials the righteous must endure before the final day of deliverance.
The Jews of Jerusalem write to their brethren in Egypt, urging them to keep the Feast of Tabernacles and recounting God's preservation of the temple's sacred fire.
Historical records describe how Jeremiah hid the tabernacle and the ark, and how Nehemiah and Judas collected sacred books for a new library.
King Seleucus sends Heliodorus to seize the temple treasury, but a terrifying heavenly vision strikes Heliodorus down and protects the sacred funds.
Jason usurps the high priesthood through bribery and introduces Greek customs, leading to the murder of the righteous Onias and general moral decay.
False rumors of the king's death lead to internal strife, prompting Antiochus to invade Jerusalem, massacre the inhabitants, and loot the temple sanctuary.
Antiochus enforces pagan worship and the desecration of the temple, highlighted by the steadfast martyrdom of the elderly scribe Eleazar.
A mother and her seven sons endure gruesome torture and execution for refusing to eat forbidden meat, testifying of their faith in a literal resurrection.
Judas Maccabeus rallies six thousand men, defeats the massive armies of Nicanor and Gorgias, and distributes the spoils among the afflicted and the poor.
While fleeing after a defeat, King Antiochus Epiphanes is struck with a painful internal disease and dies in the mountains after a desperate, late repentance.
Judas cleanses the temple and restores the altar, then proceeds to defeat the Idumeans and Timothy's forces with the help of heavenly protectors.
The governor Lysias invades Judea but is routed by Judas at Beth-zur, leading to a diplomatic exchange and a formal peace agreement.
After winning several battles, Judas discovers that fallen Jewish soldiers had carried forbidden idols, prompting him to offer a sin offering for the dead.
The young king Antiochus Eupator invades with a massive army, but after a skirmish at Modin, he agrees to terms of peace with the Jews.
The traitor Alcimus plots with Nicanor, who initially befriends Judas but is later ordered to arrest him, leading to the noble death of Razis.
Judas sees a vision of Jeremiah the prophet and defeats Nicanor in a final decisive battle, resulting in the establishment of a yearly festival.
Mordecai interprets his previous dream, realizing that the little fountain and the two dragons represent the events and people involved in the Jews' deliverance.
Mordecai, a Jewish official in the Persian court, has a symbolic dream involving dragons, darkness, and a small spring that grows into a great river.
Mordecai uncovers a plot by two eunuchs to assassinate the king, reports them, and is officially recorded in the king's chronicles for his loyalty.
The text provides the specific wording of the king's decree against the Jews, followed by Mordecai's humble prayer acknowledging God's absolute sovereignty.
Queen Esther strips off her royal robes, fasts, and offers a soul-stirring prayer to God, pleading for strength and the salvation of Israel.
Esther bravely enters the king's presence uninvited and faints from fear, but God softens the king's heart, and he lovingly reassures her of her safety.
King Artaxerxes issues a new decree denouncing the treachery of Aman and commanding his subjects to help the Jews celebrate their deliverance.
Baruch reads a book of confession to the exiles in Babylon, who then send money and the record to Jerusalem with a request for prayer and a plea for divine mercy.
The exiles acknowledge that their suffering is the fulfillment of prophetic warnings and plead for God to remember His covenant and deliver them for His name's sake.
Israel is exhorted to seek wisdom, which is described as the hidden source of all creation and life that has been revealed through the law of God.
Jerusalem, personified as a mourning mother, encourages her children in exile to remain faithful and look forward to the day when God will restore them to their home.
Jerusalem is told to put off her garments of mourning and put on the glory of God, as her children are gathered back from the east and west.
Jeremiah writes a letter to the captives in Babylon, warning them that the man-made idols of the heathen are powerless and unworthy of fear or worship.
